channel.title: Unit-Tests on Make Things Happen
channel.description: Recent content in Unit-Tests on Make Things Happen

item.title: Tricky parts when implementing unit tests for Android in Kotlin
item.description:
Testing data classes in Kotlin

As we all know, data classes are final by default on Kotlin and if you try to use Mockito and mock such a class, you will get an exception. What you need to do is add this file:

item.title: Running unit tests for LiveData with Jupiter on Android
item.description:
Running unit tests for ViewModels on Android where you have LiveData objects can be tricky. It is very possible that you get this exception:

java.lang.RuntimeException: Method getMainLooper in android.os.Looper not mocked.

item.title: Race conditions in unit tests with RxJava when using TestScheduler
item.description: I recently had a very strange bug when it comes to using a BehaviorSubject inside the object that I am testing.

item.title: Creating a SonarQube custom plugin for Kotlin
item.description: In Android we decided that we want to implement a unit test performance monitor. The reason for it is that sometimes some unit tests would execute in more than 1 second and it was all because we would use the Schedulers.trampoline() RxJava scheduler instead of the TestScheduler which is part of the RX package.

item.title: The difference between a spec and a test (Behaviour Driven Development)
item.description:
What is a spec

There was a time where I saw some tests which my colleagues made where the file name in the test folder would be: “users.spec.coffee”. So “spec” is short of specification and it is a totally different way of how we think  about tests.

item.title: Mockito anyString() vs any() – null handling
item.description:
Sometimes, when I mock a method in Mockito, I used anyString instead of any and I get into a mess and my test fails. Why?

given(mUserRepo.getUser(anyString(). any())).willReturn(new User());

And what will happen if

mUserRepo.getUser(null);

UserRepo is called with null? Well, the matcher doesn’t work and the test fails. anyString() does not work with null values. If you pass null to a mocked service then use any() as a matcher.

item.title: How to mock a dynamic value returned from a method with Mockito?
item.description: Probably, most of you already know this, but it is good to keep it in mind when you want to dynamically change the values returned by a mock you have.

item.title: Mockito verify input parameter was called with a given value
item.description:
Edit 23.04.2018
According to this issue, Mockito cannot capture var-args still.

There is a very easy way to verify a method has been called with the right parameter in Mockito. All you need to do is use captors, to capture the actual argument. Here is how you can use them:


ArgumentCaptor argument = ArgumentCaptor.forClass(RuleEntity.class);
 verify(mRulesRepository).createRule(argument.capture());
 assertEquals(1,argument.getValue().getRules().get(0).getTriggers().size())

Simple it is, just a quick reminder for starters.
